They compete in the [East of Scotland Football League](/wiki/East_of_Scotland_Football_League "East of Scotland Football League"), First Division. First team matches are played at Meadowbank Stadium.

**Leith Athletic Football Club** is a [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") area of [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland. They compete in the [East of Scotland Football League](/wiki/East_of_Scotland_Football_League "East of Scotland Football League"), First Division. First team matches are played at Meadowbank Stadium.

The present club considers itself to be a continuation of the original Leith Athletic F.C., which was founded in 1887. They played in the [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League") in four different spells between 1897 and 1953, but went out of business in 1955. The name was revived at local youth level in 1996. In 2008, Leith Athletic returned to senior football when they amalgamated with [Edinburgh Athletic](/wiki/Edinburgh_Athletic_F.C. "Edinburgh Athletic F.C.") and took the latter's place in the East of Scotland League.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leith_Athletic_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Original club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leith_Athletic_F.C.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Original club")]

Leith Athletic were founded in 1887 in the [Port of Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith"). In 1891, Leith replaced [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") side [Cowlairs](/wiki/Cowlairs_F.C. "Cowlairs F.C.") in the [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League"). After a reasonable start (fourth out of 12 in [1892](/wiki/1891%E2%80%9392_in_Scottish_football "1891–92 in Scottish football")), Leith had to apply for re-election in [1894](/wiki/1893%E2%80%9394_in_Scottish_football "1893–94 in Scottish football") and [1895](/wiki/1894%E2%80%9395_in_Scottish_football "1894–95 in Scottish football"). They received only three votes in the latter year and were relegated to the [Second Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Second_Division "Scottish Football League Second Division"). Leith fared rather better in the second flight, finishing second in [1896](/wiki/1895%E2%80%9396_in_Scottish_football "1895–96 in Scottish football"), [1897](/wiki/1896%E2%80%9397_in_Scottish_football "1896–97 in Scottish football") and 1899, but they could not muster enough votes to be elected back into the [First Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_First_Division "Scottish Football League First Division"). In 1905, having failed again in the end of [1905 season](/wiki/1904%E2%80%9305_in_Scottish_football "1904–05 in Scottish football") voting, Leith Athletic were wound up and a new limited company formed to take over the old club's assets.

In 1891, [Robert Clements](/wiki/Robert_Clements_(footballer) "Robert Clements (footballer)") and [Matt McQueen](/wiki/Matt_McQueen "Matt McQueen") played for [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") against [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_football_team_(1882%E2%80%931950) "Ireland national football team (1882–1950)") in Glasgow, McQueen having played a year earlier against [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") at Underwood Park in [Paisley](/wiki/Paisley,_Renfrewshire "Paisley, Renfrewshire"). [Geordie Anderson](/wiki/Geordie_Anderson "Geordie Anderson"), [James Blessington](/wiki/James_Blessington "James Blessington") and [Robert Laing](/w/index.php?title=Robert_Laing_(footballer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Robert Laing (footballer) (page does not exist)") would represent the [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_XI "Scottish Football League XI") against the Scottish Alliance League and the [Irish League](/wiki/Irish_League_representative_team "Irish League representative team") in the [1892–93 season](/wiki/1892%E2%80%9393_in_Scottish_football "1892–93 in Scottish football"). Blessington was transferred to [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") in June 1893 for £20, and would gain four [caps](/wiki/Cap_(sport) "Cap (sport)") for Scotland against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and Ireland.

Now playing as Leith F.C., the team comfortably won the Scottish Second Division championship in [1906](/wiki/1905%E2%80%9306_in_Scottish_football "1905–06 in Scottish football").[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1) Despite this triumph, they failed to be elected to the First Division,[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1) as runners-up [Clyde](/wiki/Clyde_F.C. "Clyde F.C.") and fourth placed [Hamilton Academical](/wiki/Hamilton_Academical_F.C. "Hamilton Academical F.C.") were preferred. Leith and [Raith Rovers](/wiki/Raith_Rovers_F.C. "Raith Rovers F.C.") finished level on points and were declared joint champions in [1910](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_in_Scottish_football "1909–10 in Scottish football").[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1) Raith were promoted, but it appears that Leith did not contest the elections. The [1912–13 season](/wiki/1912%E2%80%9313_in_Scottish_football "1912–13 in Scottish football") saw Leith finish in last position and won re-election to stay in the league. They survived until the competition was suspended in [1915](/wiki/1915%E2%80%9316_in_Scottish_football "1915–16 in Scottish football") and they joined the [Eastern League](/wiki/Eastern_Football_League_(Scotland) "Eastern Football League (Scotland)").

Leith closed down for the duration of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in 1916. When the club was reformed in 1919, the old name of Leith Athletic was revived and the club joined the Western League. After playing for one season ([1923–24](/wiki/1923%E2%80%9324_in_Scottish_football "1923–24 in Scottish football")) in the [Scottish Alliance](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Alliance "Scottish Football Alliance"), Leith were admitted to the [Third Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Third_Division "Scottish Football League Third Division") in 1924. Leith won the Third Division championship in [1926](/wiki/1925%E2%80%9326_in_Scottish_football "1925–26 in Scottish football"), but failed to win election to the Second Division.[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1) The club were eliminated on the chairman's casting vote in the third ballot. It was becoming apparent that the two most prominent [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") clubs, [Heart of Midlothian](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C.") and [Hibernian](/wiki/Hibernian_F.C. "Hibernian F.C.") were blocking attempts by Leith to progress.[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1)

The abolition of the Third Division meant that Leith had to rejoin the Scottish Alliance, but in 1927 they were admitted into the Scottish League, replacing [Nithsdale Wanderers](/wiki/Nithsdale_Wanderers_F.C. "Nithsdale Wanderers F.C."). The club's fortunes improved and they won the Second Division championship in [1930](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9330_in_Scottish_football "1929–30 in Scottish football") and promotion to the First Division. Leith survived in the top division in [1931](/wiki/1930%E2%80%9331_in_Scottish_football "1930–31 in Scottish football"), but were relegated in [1932](/wiki/1931%E2%80%9332_in_Scottish_football "1931–32 in Scottish football").[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1) The club were a middle-ranking Second Division side until the league was suspended in [1939–40](/wiki/1939%E2%80%9340_in_Scottish_football "1939–40 in Scottish football").

An attempt in 1945 to revive [St Bernard's](/wiki/St_Bernard%27s_F.C. "St Bernard's F.C."), who had lost their ground in 1943 through a merger with Leith Athletic came to nothing.[[2]](#cite_note-Crampsey131-2) In 1946, Leith became founder members of the new [Division C](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Division_C "Scottish Football League Division C") (third tier). The Second Division was expanded from 14 to 16 clubs in 1947, and Leith were elected to one of the vacancies. They were relegated in [1948](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_in_Scottish_football "1947–48 in Scottish football"), however, when the third tier was split, Leith joined Division C (North & East).

The C Divisions were made up largely of [reserve teams](/wiki/Reserve_team "Reserve team") and Leith campaigned for the non-reserve teams to be included in the Second Division. They were not successful and were expelled in August 1953, when they refused to play any more fixtures in Division C.[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1) It was too late for the club to join another competition and the club went out of business in 1955. In a supreme irony, the two C Divisions were abandoned that summer and, just as Leith had wanted, the non-reserve sides were admitted to the Second Division.[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey296-1)

### Modern club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leith_Athletic_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern club")]

A new Leith Athletic was formed in 1996, primarily as a club for boys football, with teams at various age groups between the ages of 5 and 21, whilst the club's adult side joined the [Central Scottish Amateur Football League](/wiki/Central_Scottish_Amateur_Football_League "Central Scottish Amateur Football League"). In 2005, they attained their first Scottish amateur international, when Paul Lee played in a 2–1 win against Leinster. In the same year, [Danny Swanson](/wiki/Danny_Swanson "Danny Swanson"), Douglas Thom and Shaun Woodburn were capped for the Scottish Youth Football Association under-19 team, playing against Mid Ulster and Wales.

Leith Athletic amalgamated with [Edinburgh Athletic](/wiki/Edinburgh_Athletic_F.C. "Edinburgh Athletic F.C.") in 2008 and took its place in the [East of Scotland Football League](/wiki/East_of_Scotland_Football_League "East of Scotland Football League").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Leith were promoted to the East of Scotland Premier Division in 2011 but were relegated after one season.[[4]](#cite_note-4) They regained their Premier Division place in [2013](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_East_of_Scotland_Football_League "2013–14 East of Scotland Football League") and later won the league when it became a single division in [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_East_of_Scotland_Football_League "2015–16 East of Scotland Football League").

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leith_Athletic_F.C.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stadium")]

At the time of their election to the Scottish Football League in 1891, Leith played at Bank Park, which was renamed [Beechwood Park](/wiki/Beechwood_Park_(Leith) "Beechwood Park (Leith)") in 1895. After the end of the [1898–99](/wiki/1898%E2%80%9399_Scottish_Football_League "1898–99 Scottish Football League") season the club moved to [St Bernard's](/wiki/St_Bernard%27s_F.C. "St Bernard's F.C.")' [New Logie Green](/wiki/New_Logie_Green "New Logie Green"), where they played two league matches before moving to [Hawkhill](/wiki/Hawkhill "Hawkhill") for the remainder of the [1899–1900 season](/wiki/1899%E2%80%931900_Scottish_Football_League "1899–1900 Scottish Football League").

Between 1900 and 1904 Leith played at [Chancelot Park](/wiki/Chancelot_Park "Chancelot Park"), before moving to [Old Logie Green](/wiki/Old_Logie_Green "Old Logie Green"). After the SFL was suspended during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") the club played some matches at Chancelot Park and Wardie Park. When the club returned to the SFL in 1924 they returned to Old Logie Green. At the end of the [1925–26 season](/wiki/1925%E2%80%9326_Scottish_Football_League "1925–26 Scottish Football League") the club moved again, this time to [New Powderhall](/wiki/Powderhall_Stadium "Powderhall Stadium").

After only a season at New Powderhall, the club moved to [Marine Gardens](/wiki/Marine_Gardens "Marine Gardens"), where they remained until moving to [Meadowbank](/wiki/Old_Meadowbank "Old Meadowbank") in 1936. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the ground required rebuilding, and Leith spent the [1946–47 season](/wiki/1946%E2%80%9347_Scottish_Football_League "1946–47 Scottish Football League") at the adjacent [New Meadowbank](/wiki/New_Meadowbank "New Meadowbank"). When the original Meadowbank was reopened, it was renamed [Old Meadowbank](/wiki/Old_Meadowbank "Old Meadowbank"). In the early 1950s an experimental floodlit match was played against an invitation XI including the then leading Arsenal player, Jimmy Logie.

The reformed club played at [Leith Links](/wiki/Leith_Links "Leith Links") and Muirhouse Playing Fields before moving to the Meadowbank 3G pitch adjacent to [Meadowbank Stadium](/wiki/Meadowbank_Stadium "Meadowbank Stadium") in 2013. When Meadowbank closed for redevelopment in December 2017, matches were relocated to Peffermill 3G.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Former players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leith_Athletic_F.C.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Former players")]

For a list of all Leith Athletic players with a Wikipedia article, see [Category:Leith Athletic F.C. players](/wiki/Category:Leith_Athletic_F.C._players "Category:Leith Athletic F.C. players").

Two players from the original Leith Athletic were [capped](/wiki/Cap_(football) "Cap (football)") by [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team"): [Matt McQueen](/wiki/Matt_McQueen "Matt McQueen"), who made two appearances, in 1890 and 1891; and [Robert Clements](/wiki/Robert_Clements_(footballer) "Robert Clements (footballer)"), capped once in 1891.
